default.repeat_prefix: Use the Google search screenshots to identify the user intent of the query that is most relatable to the product, assume that as the user intent, and update your final judgment accordingly.

prompts:
The attached screenshot contains a Search Experience to Product Usefulness task. The Query is on the right, the Product ad is on the left. Create the following overview:

Interpretations Table: Likelihood | Interpretation One-line description per row of what the user is probably trying to find.

Query components: table with columns Match | Term | What it is. Identify the interpretation of the query that is most relatable to the product, and treat it as the working interpretation for all subsequent assessments. Keep explanations as short as possible — ideally just a label like 'brand' or 'model nr' or 'file format'. Only expand if the term is niche, technical, or foreign-domain, in which case explain proportionally longer and in plainer language the more it relies on assumed background knowledge. For terms that require simplification: give the shortest explanation that captures the essential nature of the thing — compressed to the irreducible gist, but complete enough that someone unfamiliar has an accurate mental model of what it is.

Query Meaning In bulletpoints: One concise snapshot of what real-world results this query implies: typical products, price range, likely brands/retailers, and any hard constraints, Product Target Customer (abbreviated) (if applicable), Brand Target Customer (abbreviated) (if applicable). This is the baseline against which the product is judged.

Product Overview First line: what the product is — Category, Brand, Modifiers/Misc. Table: Match | Component | Explanation  Rows: product type, brand, color, and all other direct product attributes.

Product Assessment Table: Match | Dimension | Assessment  Rows covering all applicable indirect/contextual factors: Intent (match), Purpose (match), Function (match), Context of use (match), Market segment (match), Price tier of Product / Brand (match), Target Demographic, Important (requirement), Necessary (requirement), Substitutability, Compatibility, Closest link, Product Target Customer (abbreviated), Brand Target Customer (abbreviated). Add or omit rows as applicable.

Applicable Concepts Table: Query Type | Concept | Why Query Type = category (e.g. Product Seeking, Information Seeking). Concept = the specific pattern (e.g. Related but Wrong Purpose/Function, Acceptable Alternative). Why = one-line justification.

Then give a final overall relevance score from 0-100% based on how well the Product addresses the most plausible interpretation of the Query, and how well it satisfies necessary and important requirements implied by the Query (plus short explanation). Finally, suggest a rating (Egregious / Poor / Okay / Good) with a confidence percentage and one-line justification. Apply the rating concepts and decision rules from your instructions when identifying applicable concepts and suggesting a rating.

Format: Phrases over sentences. Cut all filler. No 'this term refers to' or 'the user is likely looking for' — just the payload. Every word must earn its place. Keep row names short (meaning omit redundant words like match or requirement). Expand all abbreviations and acronyms on first use. Flag and briefly gloss any model/platform/product-line identifiers that are not self-evident. Use ✅ / 🟡 / ❌ to visually indicate positive, mixed, or negative assessments wherever applicable.

The attached screenshot contains a Search Experience to Product Usefulness task. The Query is on the right, the Product ad is on the left. Regarding the Query: For each semantically distinct component, provide a bullet point explaining what it is. Keep explanations as short as possible — ideally just a label like 'brand name' or 'model nr' or 'file format'. Only expand if the term is niche, technical, or foreign-domain, in which case explain proportionally longer and in plainer language the more it relies on assumed background knowledge. For terms that require simplification: give the shortest explanation that captures the essential nature of the thing — compressed to the irreducible gist, but complete enough that someone unfamiliar has an accurate mental model of what it is. Then, below the bullets, list the most plausible interpretations of the full query, each with an estimated likelihood percentage and a one-line description of what the user is probably trying to find. Then assess the Product against the query: whether it addresses the most plausible interpretation, satisfies necessary and important requirements, and matches on purpose and function — not just surface topic or category. Identify which established rating concept(s) apply (e.g. 'Product Seeking – Related but Wrong Purpose/Function', 'Retailer/Brand – Acceptable Alternative') and one phrase on why. Format: Phrases over sentences. Cut all filler. No 'this term refers to' or 'the user is likely looking for' — just the payload. Every word must earn its place.Expand all abbreviations and acronyms on first use. Flag and briefly gloss any model/platform/product-line identifiers that are not self-evident. Then give a final overall relevance score from 0-100% based on how well the Product addresses the most plausible interpretation of the Query, and how well it satisfies necessary and important requirements implied by the Query (plus short explanation). Finally, suggest a rating (Egregious / Poor / Okay / Good) with a confidence percentage and one-line justification.
rules.repeat_prefix: Use the Google search screenshots to identify the user intent of the query that is most relatable to the product, assume that as the user intent, and update your final judgment accordingly.

prompts:
Evaluate if the content is Get Rich Quick (GRQ) per guidelines.

INPUT: image or text (incl. URL)

---

1) Task (1–2 lines)
- What’s offered?
- How money is implied?

(image → extract text; include only relevant visuals)

---

2) Category (if any)
Pick closest GRQ category from manual or leave blank.

---

3) Signals (only what’s present)

---

4) Decision: GRQ? → Yes / No / Unrateable
Rule: unclear/mixed → Yes

---

OUTPUT

Task:
...

Category:
...

Positive Signals:
- ...

Negative Signals:
- ...

Rating: Yes / No / Unrateable
Confidence: Low / Med / High

Why:
Signals → conclusion (no filler)
